evaluation.manager_summary: Shelby handled a hesitant caller (Megan) with exceptional empathy and rapport, acknowledging cost and readiness objections constructively before moving to logistics. Insurance details were captured and verification scheduled for same-day callback with clear next steps. Clinical screening was light—substance type and duration captured, but frequency, last use, and withdrawal risk were not probed, which may be a gap for level-of-care planning.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent immediately acknowledged Megan's emotional state, validated her difficulty in calling, and used her name within the first exchange. The tone was warm, not scripted, and matched the caller's emotional register throughout. The agent slowed down to explore Megan's hesitation meaningfully before moving to logistics.

Agent captured carrier name, member ID, and group number efficiently. Verification was framed as a helpful step, and alternatives (sliding scale, CareCredit) were proactively offered. Minor detail: plan type wasn't explicitly captured, though all other essential fields were present.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Consider capturing plan type (PPO/HMO) explicitly alongside carrier, member ID, and group number.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Substance type (alcohol) and duration (three years, 'heavily') were captured. However, the agent did not probe for frequency, last use, withdrawal risk, or prior treatment history — all standard intake screening elements. The agent stayed appropriately in intake role without diagnosing.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Probe gently for frequency of use, last drink, withdrawal history, and prior treatment attempts to ensure proper level-of-care placement.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent appropriately recognized this as a this-week rather than same-day need, scheduling a callback within hours and offering IOP as an appropriate level of care. No urgency signals (withdrawal, overdose, suicidal ideation) were present, and the agent correctly de-escalated to a structured next step without manufacturing urgency.
Caller is the patient themselves. This dimension does not apply.

Agent acknowledged both cost and readiness objections before responding, avoided defensive or salesy language, and offered concrete alternatives. The reframe of readiness ('willing to try even when you're scared') and the honest response to 'what if it doesn't work' were particularly effective and grounded.

Agent set a specific next step (admissions coordinator callback), clear timing (by 4pm, 2-4pm window), and confirmed caller availability. The purpose of the callback (benefits review and schedule walkthrough) was explicit, and ownership was clear.

Agent avoided all diagnostic language, made no treatment outcome guarantees, and stayed clearly within intake role. The statement 'no one can promise you it will' is appropriately cautious and compliant. All language was consistent with standard admissions practice.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I'm really glad you didn't talk yourself out of it today, Megan. What's going on?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I hear that. Sometimes the hardest part is making that first call, and you've already done it.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Thank you for sharing that. That's a profound moment — and you're taking it seriously, which is what matters.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Sure. I have Meridian Care. Member ID BP22149395, group GRP-588547.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Perfect. I've noted that. Now — most plans cover substance use treatment at a significant level... we also have a sliding-scale fee and financing through CareCredit.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: we have never turned someone away because of cost alone. That's not who we are.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I've been drinking pretty heavily for about three years. I know I need to stop.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I hear that. Sometimes the hardest part is making that first call, and you've already done it. What kind of reasons have been coming up for you?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Let me have our admissions coordinator call you this afternoon with your benefits information and walk you through that schedule. Can you take a call between two and four?

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: The outpatient piece matters. I can't be away from her.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Those are really honest things to say and I want to take them seriously. Let's start with the readiness piece, because I think it matters most.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: 'ready' doesn't always mean feeling confident. Sometimes it means being willing to try even when you're scared. And you're clearly willing.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: No one can promise you it will. What I can promise is that not trying guarantees the outcome you're trying to avoid.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Let me have our admissions coordinator call you this afternoon with your benefits information and walk you through that schedule. Can you take a call between two and four?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: You'll hear from us by four o'clock.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: No one can promise you it will. What I can promise is that not trying guarantees the outcome you're trying to avoid.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: 'ready' doesn't always mean feeling confident. Sometimes it means being willing to try even when you're scared.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: we'll work with you to find the right level of care — whether that's outpatient so you can be home with your daughter, or something more intensive.
